title: Mint sauce
subtitle: Fresh mint, vinegar, and sugar
excerpt: Fresh mint leaves finely chopped and dissolved in a sharp vinegar-and-sugar syrup. Sharp, clean, and the only accompaniment for roast lamb in traditional British cooking.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Mint sauce recipe.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). Mint sauce.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 hours ahead. The flavour improves as the mint macerated in the vinegar. Cover and keep at room temperature.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soaking a chopped herb or fruit in sugar, vinegar, or alcohol, which draws out the flavour and softens the texture over time.

content.text: Mint sauce is made in five minutes from three ingredients. The quality of the mint matters enormously: fresh spearmint at the height of its season is entirely different from a bunch left wilting in the fridge. The vinegar should be white wine or malt; the sharpness is the point.

Finely chop the mint leaves. Sprinkle the sugar and a pinch of salt over the mint and keep chopping until everything is finely minced together. The salt and sugar draw moisture from the mint as you chop.
Transfer to a small bowl or jug. Pour over the vinegar. Stir well. The sauce should
for at least 10 minutes before serving, which allows the vinegar to draw out the mint flavour and the sugar to dissolve completely.
Taste and adjust: add a little more vinegar for sharpness, a little more sugar for sweetness. It should be clearly sharp with a clean mint flavour.

items.symptom: The mint has turned dark brown
items.cause: Left too long after chopping without vinegar, or the mint was very mature with tough stems.
items.fix: Add the vinegar immediately after chopping. The acid in the vinegar slows discolouration. Use young mint and strip the leaves from the stems.

items.cause: Too much vinegar relative to the mint.
items.fix: Add a little more sugar and allow to macerate for another 10 minutes. More chopped mint also balances a sharp sauce.

items.cause: Not enough time to macerate, or it was not stirred properly.
items.fix: Stir vigorously when the vinegar is added. Allow a full 10 to 15 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: The sauce tastes thin and watery
items.cause: The mint was not chopped finely enough.
items.fix: The finer the mint is chopped, the more flavour is released. A very rough chop produces a weaker sauce.

content.text: Use malt vinegar instead of white wine vinegar for a sharper, more traditional version. A tablespoon of boiling water added with the vinegar dissolves the sugar faster and produces a slightly less sharp sauce. Honey instead of sugar gives a deeper sweetness.

Mint sauce is the oldest surviving British condiment still in regular use. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton's recipe is almost identical to what is made today. The combination of lamb and mint is ancient in British cooking and likely predates the Roman occupation. The commercial jarred versions have never successfully replaced the fresh-made sauce, which is why it appears on this site.
